Task: Parallel processing of nested vectors

I have a vector of vectors a : Vec<Vec<usize> in Rust. The inner vectors Vec<usize> do not have the same size.

How can I apply a function in parallel to every usize element inside a Vec<Vec<usize>> while preserving the nested structure (using e.g. par_iter?

So far, I have (unsuccesfully) tried the following

  • reshape/flatten a : Vec<Vec<usize> to b : Vec<usize>
  • apply a function to b : Vec<usize> that creates a new vector, e.g. c : Vec<String>
  • reshape c to the same size as a : Vec<Vec<String>>

How do I do that in the most elegant way?

prompt:
Task: Deserialize the same JSON field into two different struct fields

I'm trying to deserialize a JSON object, and use the value from one of its fields for two different fields in the Rust struct. I've seen similar questions to this, but all of them were trying to do something more complicated than I am.

My input JSON looks like this:

{
    "name": "Henry"
    "ac": 16,
    "hp": 54
}

And I want to deserialize it into a struct like this:

struct Character {
    name: String,
    ac: u8,
    current: u16,
    max: u16,
    temp: u16
}

Having the current and max HP as separate variables is important for the program, but they should both start at the same value: the HP field from the JSON. The temp field should be 0 when the struct is initialized, so I can easily skip that. This is what I've tried:

#[derive(Deserialize)]
struct Character {
    name: String,
    ac: u8,
    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
    current: u16,
    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
    max: u16,
    #[serde(skip)]
    temp: u16
}

And this I get this error:

warning: unreachable pattern
  --> src/main.rs:10:22
   |
 8 |    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
   |                     ---- matches all the relevant values
 9 |    current: u16,
10 |    #[serde(rename = "hp")]
   |                     ^^^^ no value can reach this
   |

thread 'main' (32) panicked at src/main.rs:18:55:
called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Error("missing field `hp`", line: 1, column: 39)

Everything I can find points to the only solutions being:

  1. Use a helper struct (I'm currently doing this and want to get rid of it to simplify the code)

  2. Implement Deserialize manually (This is above my understanding, and even if I could do it it would be more complicated than my existing code.)

I don't need to do anything fancy with the values, I just want to use the same JSON field as the value for two struct fields. Is this simply impossible with Serde, or am I missing something?

prompt:
Task: How to split a shell command into tokens in a quote-aware fashion?

The task

Given the following string¹

 one  ' two  'three    four

the required function should split it in 3 tokens (one, two three, and four), in agreement with how the bash shell does:

$ function foo { echo "$# args:"; for a in "$@"; do echo "<$a>"; done; }
$ foo  one  ' two  'three    four
3 args:
<one>
< two  three>
<four>

The context is "Build your own shell" from Codecrafters.

Question

How would I go about doing that in idiomatic, modern Rust?

My attempt

The plan

Now, my idea was to take the input string " one ' two 'three four" and processing it like this (and writing the code in Haskell, to which I'm no novice, was straightforward²):

  • split at each ':

    [" one ", " two ", "three four"],

  • enumerate the entries:

    [(0, " one "), (1, " two "), (2, "three four")],

  • for each item

    • if its even-indexed (i.e. originally not quoted), group spaces and non-spaces in the string and throw away the index:

      [" ", "one", " "]

    • if its odd-indexed (those that were originally quoted), wrap it in a singleton list:

      [" two "]

    [[" ", "one", " "], [" two "], ["three", " ", "four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two ", "three", " ", "four"]

  • group together the entries that not entirely made of spaces:

    [[" "], ["one"], [" "], [" two ", "three"], [" "], ["four"]]

  • concatenate/join/flatten the sublists:

    [" ", "one", " ", " two three", " ", "four"]

  • filter out the entries entirely made of spaces:

    ["one", " two three", "four"]

The code

It took me quite a while to encode that in a Rust program that compiles (it slightly diverges from the plan, but that's not a big deal³):

use itertools::Itertools;

fn tokenize(s: &str) -> Vec<String> {
    s.split("'")
     .enumerate()
     .map(|(i, s)| if i % 2 == 0 {
         s.chars()
          .group_by(|ch| *ch == ' ')
          .into_iter()
          .map(|(is_space, chars)| (is_space, chars.collect()))
          .collect()
     } else {
         std::iter::once((false, s.to_owned())).collect::<Vec<_>>()
     })
     .flatten()
     .group_by(|(is_space, _)| !is_space)
     .into_iter()
     .map(|(_, grp)|
         grp.map(|(_, c)| c)
            .into_iter()
            .join(""))
     .filter(|s| s.chars().any(|c| c != ' '))
     .collect::<Vec<_>>()
}

pub fn main() {
    tokenize(" 'one'  ' two  'three    four")
        .iter()
        .for_each(|a| println!("`{}`", a));
}
Concerns and doubts

And I'm happy to see that it turned out to be correct as soon as I managed to make it compile it, but I have to admit that every single .into_iter()/.iter()/.collect()/.collect::<something>()/.to_owned(), I've added it precisely to make the compiler happy, without actually fully understanding what I'm doing.

Now, in hindsight I understand the reasons for some of those, also thanks to my experience with C++ Ranges library, e.g. I totally understand why the final .collect::<Vec<_>>() is necessary.

On the other hand, being new to Rust, I'm not sure how esoteric the code above looks to rustaceans.

(¹) I'm taking it as a relatively general example because it contains a single quoted portion, which is immediately followed by non-whitespace characters, but preceded by whitespace characters, and because the amount of adjacent whitespaces is not 1 in the non-quoted portion; oh, and the leading portion of the string has a leading space.

(²) Here's the complete Haskell code to do the above:

module Main where

import Data.List
import Data.List.Split
import Data.Function

main :: IO ()
main = getLine >>= print . splitIntoTokens

splitIntoTokens :: String -> [String]
splitIntoTokens = filter (not . allSpaces)
                . map concat
                . groupBy (both (not . allSpaces))
                . concatMap (\(i, s) -> if even i
                                          then groupBy ((==) `on` (== ' ')) s
                                          else pure s)
                . enumerate
                . splitOn singleQuote
                where
                  allSpaces = all (== ' ')
                  both = on (&&)
                  singleQuote = "'"
                  enumerate = zip [0 :: Int ..]

(³) That's because Haskell's and Rust's group_by have different semantics and API: the former's group_by takes a binary predicate and throws away the result of the predicate after the grouping is done, the latter takes a unary function (like Haskell's groupOn, and returns the function's return value paired with each group).

prompt:
Task: Design dilemma with enum_dispatch in Rust

I'm designing my system which consists of three parts, abstract layer (al) where traits and generic data structures are defined; implementation layer (il) where different structures implement traits; and business layer (bl) where the system runs with given configurations.

Now, the il depends the al because of traits. Meanwhile, the bl depends on both al and il because it uses the public interfaces and specific structures. Now there is a cycle upsetting me.

In the al, there's a trait named Rest which defines lots of network service and it's annotated with #[enum_dispatch]. In the il, both Foo and Boo implement Rest. In the bl, I intend to use enum_dispatch to achieve static dispatch as below:

#[enum_dispatch(Rest)]
pub enum Item {
    Foo,
    Boo
}

If it worked, I'm allowed to directly use methods within the trait on different variants as like dynamic dispatch but with much better performance. However, enum_dispatch requires the annotated trait and enum must be defined within the same crate. If so, I have to integrate the al and the bl, violating my purpose to separate logic. And even if I integrated them to one crate, which we still call al, it's still very tricky because now the al and the il depend on each other, there'll be multiple different versions of crate al in the dependency graph, making it a circle.

Is there any solution that I can still keep a separate hierarchy without integrating the three crates into one? It's almost impossible to manually implement Rest for the enum either because this trait contains almost 20 methods.

Task: "One lifetime is more general than the other" when accepting async function as an argument

I have the following code:

use std::future::Future;

async fn simple_test(string: &mut String) {
    string.push_str("test result\n");
}

async fn harness<T, Fut>(test: T)
where
    T: Fn(&mut String) -> Fut,
    Fut: Future<Output = ()>
{
    let mut string = format!("");
    test(&mut string).await;
    println!("new string is:\n{string}");
}

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    harness(simple_test).await;
}

In this code, simple_test is an async function representing a test case (for another bit of software that I'm writing tests for). simple_test accepts a mutable reference to a String, and writes the test result to the String. (In my real code that I created this MWE from, the argument is a custom logger type.)

harness is a wrapper function that I want to apply to every test case function. I want harness to instantiate a logger, write a preamble to it, call the test function using the logger as an argument, and then write a post-amble and return.

However, this code doesn't compile. Rust gives this error:

error[E0308]: mismatched types
   --> src/main.rs:180:5
    |
180 |     harness(simple_test).await;
    |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ one type is more general than the other
    |
    = note: expected opaque type `impl for<'a> Future<Output = ()>`
               found opaque type `impl Future<Output = ()>`
    = help: consider `await`ing on both `Future`s
    = note: distinct uses of `impl Trait` result in different opaque types
note: the lifetime requirement is introduced here
   --> src/main.rs:170:27
    |
170 |     T: Fn(&mut String) -> Fut,
    |                           ^^^

error: implementation of `Fn` is not general enough
   --> src/main.rs:180:5
    |
168 | / async fn harness<T, Fut>(test: T)
169 | | where
170 | |     T: Fn(&mut String) -> Fut,
    | |        ---------------------- doesn't satisfy where-clause
171 | |     Fut: Future<Output = ()>
    | |____________________________- due to a where-clause on `harness`...
...
180 |       harness(simple_test).await;
    |       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    |
    = note: ...`for<'a> fn(&'a mut String) -> impl Future<Output = ()> {simple_test}` must implement `Fn<(&mut String,)>`
    = note: ...but it actually implements `Fn<(&'0 mut String,)>`, for some specific lifetime `'0`

What I've tried

I have tried applying solutions from several other sources, but I don't really understand the other sources well enough to do anything other than copy-pasting text.

  • From this answer, I tried re-writing my main function as harness(|s: &mut String| simple_test(s)).await;. Then I get a complaint about lifetime issues. I can't seem to fix this complaint, and I also don't really want to because I want the syntax for calling the handler to be very simple (ideally no more complicated than what's in my initial code):
   Compiling handler-test v0.1.0 (/Users/erysdam/RealHome/handler-test)
error: lifetime may not live long enough
   --> src/main.rs:180:30
    |
180 |     harness(|s: &mut String| simple_test(s)).await;
    |                 -          - ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ returning this value requires that `'1` must outlive `'2`
    |                 |          |
    |                 |          return type of closure `impl Future<Output = ()>` contains a lifetime `'2`
    |                 let's call the lifetime of this reference `'1`

error: could not compile `handler-test` (bin "handler-test") due to 1 previous error
  • I can add more examples later if it would be helpful, but suffice it to say that no other answer has worked for me and I probably just need the situation explained to me from scratch.

My question

How do I fix the error? I want to call the harness function on each async test function.

Task: Decoding SCEP PKIOperation using rust CMS crate

I'm trying to decode PKCS7 binary data from a SCEP PKIOperation POST request... I'm using the CMS crate.

I can decode the data into the ContentInfo struct easily:

let ci = ContentInfo::from_der(&body).unwrap();

but when I attempt to decode the inner signedData:

let sd: SignedData = ci.content.decode_as().unwrap();

I run into the following error:

thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at src/web.rs:218:51:

called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Error { kind: Value { tag: Tag(0x02: INTEGER) }, position: Some(Length(129)) }

note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace

The following openssl command: openssl cms -inform DER -in ci.der -cmsout -print

yields the following structure:
